title: To believe in God
commentary: Belief in God is the foundation of all relationship with Him, expressed in both knowing and trusting.

title: To acknowledge the Unity of God
commentary: The declaration of God's unity establishes who He is and frames all worship and understanding.

title: To love God
commentary: This is central to both traditions, expressing devotion through heart, soul, and strength.

title: To fear God
commentary: Fear of God reflects reverence and awareness of His authority, guiding how one lives and responds.

title: To serve God
commentary: Serving God is expressed through worship, obedience, and the offering of one’s life in devotion.

title: To cleave to God
commentary: Cleave expresses a continual attachment to God, remaining connected in life and purpose.

title: On taking an oath by God’s Name
commentary: Invoking God’s Name in an oath reflects the seriousness of truth and accountability before Him.

title: On walking in God’s ways
commentary: Walking in God’s ways reflects a life shaped by His character and expressed through daily actions.

title: On Sanctifying God’s Name
commentary: Sanctifying God’s Name is expressed through conduct that reflects His holiness before others.

title: On reciting the Sh’ma each morning and evening
commentary: Regular recitation reinforces continual awareness of God and embeds His words into daily life.

title: On studying and teaching Torah
commentary: The transmission of Torah depends on both learning and passing it on faithfully from one generation to the next.

title: On binding Tefillin on the head
commentary: This command expresses the call to keep God’s words before the mind and to live with conscious remembrance of Him.

title: On binding Tefillin on the hand
commentary: This command points to the joining of devotion and action, bringing God’s instruction into what one does.

title: On making Tzitzit with thread of blue, garments corners
commentary: Tzitzit serve as a visible reminder to remember God’s commandments and walk in covenant faithfulness.

title: On affixing a Mezuzah to doorposts and gates
commentary: The mezuzah marks the household as a place ordered by God’s word and keeps His instruction at the threshold of daily life.

title: On assembling each 7th year to hear the Torah read
commentary: Gathering to hear the Torah reinforces shared understanding and renews commitment across the community.

title: On that a king must write a copy of Torah for himself
commentary: Leadership is anchored in submission to God’s instruction, requiring continual engagement with His word.

title: On that everyone should have a Torah scroll
commentary: Having the Torah present ensures that God’s instruction remains accessible and active among the people.

title: On praising God after eating, grace after meals
commentary: Giving thanks after provision acknowledges God as the source and keeps gratitude central to daily life.

title: On building a Sanctuary (Tabernacle/Temple) for God
commentary: The sanctuary represents a place set apart for God’s presence, shaping how worship and nearness are understood.

title: On respecting the Sanctuary
commentary: Respect for the sanctuary reflects recognition of what is set apart and devoted to God.

title: On guarding the Sanctuary
commentary: Guarding the sanctuary emphasizes responsibility to preserve what is holy and entrusted to care.

title: On Levitical services in the Tabernacle
commentary: Levitical service establishes order in worship, structuring how duties are carried out before God.

title: On Cohanim washing hands & feet before entering Temple
commentary: Preparation through cleansing reflects the need for purity when approaching what is set apart.

title: On kindling the Menorah by the Cohanim
commentary: The continual lighting of the menorah reflects ongoing service and the presence of light within the sanctuary.

title: On the Cohanim blessing Israel
commentary: The priestly blessing expresses God’s favor being spoken over the people through appointed service.

title: On the Showbread before the Ark
commentary: The showbread represents continual provision and presence before God within the sanctuary.

title: On burning the incense on the Golden Altar twice daily
commentary: The continual offering of incense reflects ongoing prayer and devotion rising before God.

title: On the perpetual fire on the Altar
commentary: The continual fire symbolizes ongoing devotion and the unceasing nature of service before God.

title: On removing the ashes from the Altar
commentary: Even the removal of ashes reflects ordered service, maintaining what is set apart for worship.

title: On removing unclean persons from the camp
commentary: Separation from uncleanness preserves the integrity of what is set apart and maintains the holiness of the community.

title: On honoring the Cohanim
commentary: Those appointed to sacred service are to be regarded with honor because of their role in what is set apart.

title: On the garments of the Cohanim
commentary: Priestly garments reflect distinction and identity in service, marking what is set apart for sacred purpose.

title: On Cohanim bearing the Ark on their shoulders
commentary: Carrying what is set apart requires care and reverence, emphasizing responsibility in handling sacred things.

title: On the holy anointing oil
commentary: Anointing marks what is set apart, signifying consecration for a specific purpose in service.

title: On the Cohanim ministering in rotation/watches
commentary: Structured service ensures that responsibilities are carried out in order, preserving continuity in what is set apart.

title: On the Cohanim being defiled for dead relatives
commentary: Even within sacred service, allowances are made for family, balancing holiness with human responsibility.

title: On that Cohen haGadol may only marry a virgin
commentary: The requirements for the high priest emphasize a heightened level of separation, reflecting the role’s distinct holiness.

title: On the twice daily burnt (tamid) offerings
commentary: The continual offering establishes an ongoing rhythm of devotion, marking daily life with regular dedication.

title: On the Cohen haGadol’s twice daily meal offering
commentary: The high priest’s offering reflects personal participation in devotion, not only oversight of it.

title: On the Shabbat additional (musaf) offering
commentary: Additional offerings on the Sabbath mark it as distinct, reinforcing its role as a set-apart time.

title: On the New Moon (Rosh Chodesh) additional offering
commentary: The new moon offering marks the beginning of cycles, recognizing renewal within the flow of time.

title: On the Pesach additional offering
commentary: Festival offerings reinforce remembrance, tying present worship to past acts of redemption.

title: On the second day of Pesach meal offering of the Omer
commentary: The offering of firstfruits acknowledges provision at the beginning, setting apart what comes first.

title: On the Shavuot additional (musaf) offering
commentary: The Shavuot offering marks completion of a cycle, gathering the people in a shared moment of recognition.

title: On the two loaves wave offering on Shavuot
commentary: The two loaves offering represents the presentation of firstfruits, marking completion and dedication of provision.

title: On the Rosh Hashanah additional offering
commentary: The trumpet day marks a call to remembrance, signaling a transition and drawing attention to what is set apart.

title: On the Yom Kippur additional offering
commentary: The additional offering emphasizes the seriousness of atonement, focusing on cleansing and restoration.

title: On the service of Yom Kippur (Avodah)
commentary: The Yom Kippur service represents a unique moment of approach, centered on atonement and restoration.

title: On the Sukkot additional (musaf) offerings
commentary: The Sukkot offerings reflect celebration and provision, marking a time of gathering and shared recognition.

title: On the Shemini Atzeret additional offering
commentary: Shemini Atzeret marks a distinct concluding assembly, carrying the sense of completion and held presence after the festival cycle.

title: On the three annual festival pilgrimages to the Temple
commentary: The pilgrimage festivals gather the people in shared worship, tying sacred time to sacred place.

title: On appearing before YHVH during the Festivals
commentary: Appearing before YHVH during the festivals emphasizes intentional presence, gathering before Him at appointed times.

title: On rejoicing on the Festivals
commentary: Festival joy is not incidental but commanded, making rejoicing itself part of sacred observance.

title: On the 14th of Nisan slaughtering the Pesach lamb
commentary: The slaughtering of the Pesach lamb anchors remembrance in action, linking redemption to a specific appointed act in time.

title: On eating the roasted Pesach lamb on the night of Nisan 14/15
commentary: Eating the Pesach lamb makes remembrance participatory, joining the people to the act of redemption in a tangible way.

title: On slaughtering the Pesach Sheini offering on Iyyar 14
commentary: Pesach Sheini reflects provision for those who could not participate at the appointed time, preserving access without removing the appointment.

title: On eating the Pesach Sheini lamb with matzah and maror
commentary: Pesach Sheini preserves the same pattern of participation, joining remembrance with the appointed elements.

title: On using trumpets for feast sacrifices and for times of distress
commentary: The trumpets function as signals of gathering, worship, and alarm, marking moments that call for attention before God.

title: On the minimum age of cattle to be offered
commentary: The requirement of fitness in offerings reflects that what is brought before God is to meet what has been set apart as acceptable.

title: On offering only unblemished sacrifices
commentary: Offerings are required to be without defect, reflecting what is considered acceptable and set apart.

title: On bringing salt with every offering
commentary: Salt accompanies offerings as a sign of preservation and covenant, marking what is set apart.

title: On the burnt-offering
commentary: The burnt-offering represents complete dedication, being wholly given over as an offering.

title: On the sin-offering
commentary: The sin-offering addresses wrongdoing, providing a means for atonement within the system of offerings.

title: On the guilt-offering
commentary: The guilt-offering deals with specific offenses requiring restoration, addressing both wrongdoing and its consequences.

title: On the peace-offering
commentary: The peace-offering expresses fellowship and shared participation, marking a state of peace in relation to God.

title: On the meal-offering
commentary: The meal-offering consists of produce rather than animal sacrifice, reflecting dedication of provision.

title: On offerings for a court that has erred
commentary: Provision is made for communal error in leadership, recognizing responsibility at the collective level.

title: On a fixed sin-offering for unintentional transgression
commentary: A sin-offering is prescribed for unintentional wrongdoing, addressing actions done without awareness.

title: On a guilt-offering in cases of uncertainty
commentary: A guilt-offering is brought when there is uncertainty, acknowledging responsibility even without full clarity.

title: On a guilt-offering for wrongdoing such as misappropriation
commentary: A guilt-offering addresses wrongdoing involving loss or misuse, often paired with restoration.

title: On bringing an offering according to one’s means
commentary: Provision is made for different levels of means, ensuring that participation is accessible to all.

title: On confessing one’s sins and turning from them
commentary: Confession acknowledges wrongdoing openly, forming part of the process of turning back and restoration.

title: On an offering brought by a man after discharge
commentary: An offering marks the transition from a state of impurity back into participation within the community.

title: On an offering brought by a woman after discharge
commentary: This offering reflects restoration after a period of impurity, allowing re-entry into normal participation.

title: On an offering brought by a woman after childbirth
commentary: An offering follows childbirth, marking a transition back into regular participation after a period of separation.

title: On an offering brought after cleansing from leprosy
commentary: After cleansing, an offering marks restoration, allowing full return to the community.

title: On the tithe of cattle
commentary: The tithe sets apart a portion of increase, marking recognition of provision and dedication.

title: On setting apart the firstborn of clean cattle
commentary: The firstborn is set apart, recognizing what comes first as belonging in a special way.

title: On redeeming the firstborn of man
commentary: Redemption of the firstborn reflects substitution, preserving the principle of dedication while allowing continuation of life.

title: On redeeming the firstling of a donkey
commentary: The firstling of a donkey is redeemed, preserving the principle of setting apart the first while providing an alternative to sacrifice.

title: On breaking the neck of the firstling of a donkey if not redeemed
commentary: If not redeemed, the firstling is not used, emphasizing that what is set apart cannot be treated as ordinary.

title: On bringing due offerings without delay
commentary: Offerings are to be brought without delay, reflecting promptness in fulfilling what is owed.

title: On bringing offerings only to the chosen place
commentary: Offerings are restricted to the chosen place, centralizing worship and maintaining order in how offerings are brought.

title: On bringing offerings from outside the land to the sanctuary
commentary: Offerings brought from outside the land are still directed to the sanctuary, maintaining the central place of worship.

title: On redeeming blemished sanctified animal offerings
commentary: A blemished sanctified animal is redeemed rather than offered, preserving the standard of what is acceptable.

title: On the holiness of substituted animal offerings
commentary: When an animal is substituted, both it and its replacement are regarded as set apart, maintaining the sanctity of what was designated.

title: On the Cohanim eating the remainder of the meal offerings
commentary: Portions of certain offerings are designated for the Cohanim, providing for those serving in the sanctuary.

title: On the Cohanim eating portions of sin and guilt offerings
commentary: Certain offerings are consumed by the Cohanim, linking their service with participation in the atonement process.

title: On burning consecrated offerings that become unclean
commentary: Offerings that become unclean are not used but destroyed, preserving the boundary between what is holy and what is not.

title: On burning the remainder of consecrated offerings not eaten in time
commentary: Portions of offerings not eaten within the allotted time are burned, maintaining the boundaries set for what is holy.

title: On a Nazirite letting his hair grow during separation
commentary: The Nazirite vow includes visible signs of separation, marking a period set apart for dedication.

title: On a Nazirite completing the vow with shaving and offerings
commentary: Completion of the Nazirite vow is marked by specific actions, signaling the end of the period of separation.

title: On honoring one’s vows and oaths
commentary: Vows and oaths are to be upheld, reflecting integrity in what is spoken.

title: On the authority to annul vows according to Torah
commentary: The annulment of vows is regulated, placing authority within defined boundaries.

title: On impurity from touching certain animal carcasses
commentary: Contact with certain carcasses results in a state of impurity, marking boundaries around what is handled.

title: On impurity from touching specified creeping creatures
commentary: Specific creatures are identified whose contact transmits impurity, defining clear limits.

title: On impurity of food and drink through contact with unclean things
commentary: Impurity can transfer to food and drink, extending boundaries beyond direct contact with persons.

title: On impurity during a woman’s menstruation
commentary: A defined period of impurity is associated with the menstrual cycle, structuring times of separation.

title: On impurity after childbirth
commentary: Childbirth is followed by a period of impurity, marking a transition before full return to normal participation.

title: On impurity associated with a skin disease
commentary: Certain skin conditions place a person in a state of impurity, requiring recognition and separation.

title: On impurity in garments affected by a skin condition
commentary: Impurity can affect garments, showing that certain conditions extend beyond the person to objects.

title: On impurity in a house affected by a skin condition
commentary: Impurity can extend to a dwelling, indicating that certain conditions affect the environment itself.

title: On impurity from bodily discharge in a man
commentary: Certain bodily discharges result in a state of impurity, with defined boundaries and responses.

title: On impurity related to seminal emission
commentary: Specific bodily emissions are associated with impurity, defining conditions and required responses.

title: On impurity from prolonged discharge in a woman
commentary: A prolonged discharge results in a defined state of impurity, with boundaries governing contact and restoration.

title: On impurity from a human corpse
commentary: Contact with a human corpse results in a state of impurity, marking one of the most severe forms of impurity.

title: On purification through the water of sprinkling
commentary: Water used for purification is applied in specific cases, providing a means of restoration from impurity.

title: On immersing in water to become clean
commentary: Immersion in water marks a transition from impurity to a state of cleanness in defined situations.

title: On the procedure for cleansing from a skin disease
commentary: Cleansing from certain conditions follows a defined procedure, marking restoration and re-entry.

title: On shaving as part of cleansing from a skin condition
commentary: Shaving is part of the prescribed process of cleansing, marking transition and renewal.

title: On making a person with a skin condition distinguishable
commentary: Visible signs mark a state of impurity, allowing recognition and maintaining boundaries within the community.

title: On the ashes of the red heifer for purification
commentary: Ashes prepared in a specific way are used in purification, especially in cases involving contact with death.

title: On the valuation of a person dedicated to the temple
commentary: A person dedicated by vow is assigned a set valuation, providing a structured way to fulfill the dedication.

title: On the valuation of an unclean animal dedicated to the temple
commentary: Animals not suitable for offering are valued instead, maintaining the principle of dedication through substitution.

title: On the valuation of a house dedicated to the temple
commentary: A house dedicated by vow is assigned a value, providing a structured way to fulfill the dedication.

title: On the valuation of a field dedicated to the temple
commentary: Fields are valued according to set measures, linking dedication with a defined system of assessment.

title: On restitution with an added fifth for misuse of sacred property
commentary: Misuse of what is set apart requires restitution plus an additional amount, reinforcing accountability.

title: On dedicating the fourth year fruit
commentary: The fourth year’s produce is treated as dedicated, marking a stage in the cycle of growth and use.

title: On leaving the corners of the field for the poor
commentary: Leaving portions of the harvest provides for those in need, integrating provision into the system.

title: On leaving gleanings of the field for the poor
commentary: Gleanings are left intentionally, providing ongoing access to food for those in need.

title: On leaving the forgotten sheaf for the poor
commentary: What is overlooked is not reclaimed, but left for those in need.

title: On leaving malformed grape clusters for the poor
commentary: Even imperfect produce is left available, reinforcing provision without discrimination.

title: On leaving grape gleanings for the poor
commentary: Remnants of the harvest are left accessible, ensuring continued provision.

title: On bringing firstfruits to the sanctuary
commentary: The first of the produce is brought as an offering, marking the beginning as set apart.

title: On setting aside the heave-offering
commentary: A portion is set apart and given, supporting those who serve in the sanctuary.

title: On setting aside the first tithe for the Levites
commentary: The first tithe is given to the Levites, establishing a structured provision for those without land inheritance.

title: On setting aside the second tithe
commentary: A second tithe is set apart and used in a designated place, linking provision with participation.

title: On the Levites giving a tenth of their tithe
commentary: The Levites also give a portion, maintaining a layered system of provision.

title: On setting aside the tithe for the poor in designated years
commentary: In specific years, the tithe is directed toward those in need, integrating care into the cycle.

title: On making a declaration when separating the tithes
commentary: A spoken declaration accompanies the separation of tithes, affirming that they have been handled properly.

title: On making a declaration when bringing firstfruits
commentary: Bringing firstfruits includes a declaration that recounts origins and acknowledges provision.

title: On giving the first portion of dough
commentary: A portion of dough is set apart and given, extending the pattern of first portions into daily life.

title: On leaving the produce of the sabbatical year ownerless
commentary: During the sabbatical year, produce is not held as private property, allowing access for all.

title: On resting the land in the sabbatical year
commentary: The land itself is given rest, marking a recurring interruption in regular agricultural activity.

title: On sanctifying the Jubilee year
commentary: The Jubilee year is set apart as a time of release, marking a distinct cycle within the broader system.

title: On proclaiming the Jubilee with the shofar and releasing slaves
commentary: The Jubilee is publicly announced, and those in servitude are released, marking a reset in status.

title: On the return of land to ancestral owners in the Jubilee
commentary: Land reverts to original families, restoring inherited structure and preventing permanent transfer.

title: On redeeming a house within a set period after sale
commentary: A defined window allows redemption of a house, introducing time limits into property transfer.

title: On counting the years leading to the Jubilee
commentary: The Jubilee is reached through a counted cycle, structuring time toward periodic reset.

title: On releasing debts in the sabbatical year
commentary: Debts are released during the sabbatical cycle, preventing long-term accumulation of obligation.

title: On distinctions in debt collection from foreigners
commentary: The law distinguishes between internal release and external obligations, maintaining defined boundaries.

title: On the priestly portions from the slaughter of clean animals
commentary: Specific portions from slaughter are set aside for the priests, establishing provision within ordinary consumption.

title: On giving the first of the fleece to the priest
commentary: The first of the fleece is given, extending the pattern of first portions beyond produce to livestock.

title: On items devoted by vow being given as designated
commentary: What is devoted by vow is transferred according to its designation, reflecting the binding nature of such commitments.

title: On slaughtering animals before eating
commentary: Animals are slaughtered in a prescribed way before consumption, establishing a defined process.

title: On covering the blood of a hunted animal or bird
commentary: Blood is covered after slaughter, marking a boundary in how life is handled.

title: On releasing the parent bird when taking young
commentary: When taking young, the parent is released, introducing restraint into use of animals.

title: On identifying permitted animals by their signs
commentary: Animals are identified by specific signs to determine what may be eaten.

title: On identifying permitted birds by their designation
commentary: Birds are distinguished for consumption according to defined criteria.

title: On identifying permitted locusts by their signs
commentary: Certain locusts are identified by defined signs, completing the classification of permitted creatures.

title: On identifying permitted fish by their signs
commentary: Fish are distinguished by specific signs, establishing criteria for what may be eaten.

title: On establishing the new month and ordering the calendar
commentary: Time is structured through the recognition of months and seasons, forming the basis for appointed times.

title: On resting on the seventh day
commentary: The seventh day is marked by rest, establishing a recurring rhythm in time.

title: On setting apart the Sabbath at its beginning and end
commentary: The Sabbath is set apart through acknowledgment at its boundaries, marking it as distinct in time.

title: On removing leaven before Passover
commentary: Leaven is removed in preparation, marking a transition into the appointed time.

title: On recounting the exodus on the night of Passover
commentary: The exodus is recounted, preserving memory through spoken transmission.

title: On eating unleavened bread on the first night of Passover
commentary: Unleavened bread is eaten as part of the observance, linking food with the appointed time.

title: On resting on the first day of Passover
commentary: The first day is marked by rest, setting it apart within the festival.

title: On resting on the seventh day of Passover
commentary: The final day is also marked by rest, framing the festival with defined boundaries.

title: On removing leaven before Passover
commentary: Leaven is removed in preparation, marking a transition into the appointed time.

title: On recounting the exodus on the night of Passover
commentary: The exodus is recounted, preserving memory through spoken transmission.

title: On eating unleavened bread on the first night of Passover
commentary: Unleavened bread is eaten as part of the observance, linking food with the appointed time.

title: On resting on the first day of Passover
commentary: The first day is marked by rest, setting it apart within the festival.

title: On resting on the seventh day of Passover
commentary: The final day is also marked by rest, framing the festival with defined boundaries.

title: On resting on the first day of Sukkot
commentary: The first day of Sukkot is marked by rest, setting it apart within the festival.

title: On resting on the eighth day assembly
commentary: An additional day is marked by rest, concluding the festival period with a distinct gathering.

title: On dwelling in booths during Sukkot
commentary: Temporary dwellings are used during the festival, creating a lived expression of the appointed time.

title: On taking the four species during Sukkot
commentary: Specific items are taken as part of the observance, adding physical expression to the festival.

title: On hearing the sound of the shofar
commentary: The sounding of the shofar marks the appointed time, signaling its beginning.

title: On each male giving a half-shekel for the sanctuary
commentary: A fixed contribution is given, linking individuals to the collective support of the sanctuary.

title: On heeding the prophet
commentary: Instruction delivered through a prophet is to be received, establishing a channel of guidance.

title: On appointing a king
commentary: A king may be established, creating a defined form of leadership within the community.

title: On following the decisions of the court
commentary: Decisions issued by the court are followed, maintaining order and consistency in judgment.

title: On following the majority in judgment
commentary: In matters of judgment, decisions follow the majority, establishing a consistent method for resolving disputes.

title: On punishing false witnesses as they intended for the accused
commentary: False witnesses are punished according to the harm they intended, establishing proportional justice within the legal system.

title: On the procedure for an unsolved killing
commentary: A defined procedure is followed when a killing is unresolved, acknowledging responsibility within the community.

title: On establishing cities of refuge
commentary: Cities of refuge are established, providing protection within the justice system.

title: On giving cities to the Levites
commentary: Cities are given to the Levites, providing habitation for those without a land inheritance.

title: On removing hazards from one’s property
commentary: Potential dangers are addressed, extending responsibility to the safety of others.

title: On removing idolatrous sites and objects
commentary: Idolatrous sites and objects are removed, establishing boundaries in worship and practice.

title: On the procedure for an unsolved killing
commentary: A defined procedure is followed when a killing is unresolved, acknowledging responsibility within the community.

title: On establishing cities of refuge
commentary: Cities of refuge are established, providing protection within the justice system.

title: On giving cities to the Levites
commentary: Cities are given to the Levites, providing habitation for those without a land inheritance.

title: On removing hazards from one’s property
commentary: Potential dangers are addressed, extending responsibility to the safety of others.

title: On removing idolatrous sites and objects
commentary: Idolatrous sites and objects are removed, establishing boundaries in worship and practice.

title: On appointing roles and exemptions in time of war
commentary: Specific roles are assigned and certain individuals are exempted, structuring participation in war.

title: On maintaining sanitation within the camp
commentary: The camp is kept orderly and sanitary, extending discipline beyond combat.

title: On including tools for sanitation among equipment
commentary: Necessary tools are included as part of preparation, ensuring proper maintenance of the camp.

title: On giving to the poor according to need
commentary: Assistance is given based on need, reinforcing responsibility toward others.

title: On giving provision to a servant upon release
commentary: Provision is given at release, supporting transition back into independent life.

title: On lending to the poor without interest
commentary: Loans to the poor are given without interest, reducing burden and preserving dignity.

title: On lending with interest to a foreigner
commentary: A distinction is made in lending practices, defining different terms across boundaries.

title: On returning a pledge when needed by its owner
commentary: A pledge is returned when needed, balancing obligation with compassion.

title: On paying wages on time
commentary: Wages are paid promptly, ensuring fairness in labor and livelihood.

title: On allowing a worker to eat from what he is harvesting
commentary: A worker may partake of the produce being worked, ensuring immediate provision during labor.

title: On helping unload a burdened animal
commentary: Assistance is given to relieve a burdened animal, extending care beyond ownership.

title: On helping load a burdened animal
commentary: Assistance is given in lifting burdens, reinforcing responsibility toward others.

title: On returning lost property to its owner
commentary: Lost property is restored to its owner, maintaining trust and accountability within the community.

title: On correcting wrongdoing
commentary: Wrongdoing is addressed directly, reinforcing responsibility within relationships.

title: On loving one’s neighbor as oneself
commentary: Care for others is expressed in relation to oneself, establishing a baseline for social interaction.

title: On loving the stranger
commentary: Care extends beyond familiar relationships, including those outside one’s immediate group.

title: On maintaining honest weights and measures
commentary: Accuracy in measurement is required, ensuring fairness in exchange and commerce.

title: On honoring the aged
commentary: Respect is shown to those advanced in age, recognizing their place within the community.

title: On honoring one’s parents
commentary: Parents are honored, establishing a foundational relationship within the family structure.

title: On revering one’s parents
commentary: Parents are held in reverence, reinforcing respect within the family structure.

title: On being fruitful and multiplying
commentary: The continuation and growth of life is established as a foundational aspect of humanity.

title: On the formation of marriage
commentary: Marriage is defined as a recognized relationship, forming a core unit within the family structure.

title: On a husband devoting time to his wife
commentary: A period is set aside for a husband to focus on his wife, strengthening the marital relationship.

title: On circumcising a male child
commentary: A physical sign is established within the family, marking identity and continuity.

title: On a brother marrying a childless widow
commentary: A provision is made to preserve family continuity when a man dies without children.

title: On releasing a widow from this obligation
commentary: A formal process allows release from the obligation, establishing an alternative path.

title: On marriage obligations following violation
commentary: Specific obligations are established following a violation, defining responsibility within the law.

title: On consequences for falsely accusing a spouse
commentary: False accusations within marriage carry defined consequences, protecting integrity in the relationship.

title: On legal consequences for seduction
commentary: The law defines responsibility and consequences in cases of seduction.

title: On treating a captive woman according to prescribed regulations
commentary: Specific regulations govern how a captive woman is to be treated, defining conduct in such circumstances.

title: On formalizing divorce through a written document
commentary: Divorce is formalized through a written document, establishing a defined legal process.

title: On the procedure for a suspected adulteress
commentary: A defined procedure addresses suspicion within marriage, structuring resolution under the law.

title: On administering corporal punishment for certain offenses
commentary: Certain offenses carry defined penalties, applied within the judicial system.

title: On exile to a city of refuge for unintentional killing
commentary: Exile to a place of refuge is established as a response to unintentional killing.

title: On execution by the sword for certain offenses
commentary: Specific offenses carry defined forms of execution within the judicial system.

title: On execution by strangulation for certain offenses
commentary: Certain offenses are assigned a defined method of execution under the law.

title: On execution by burning for certain offenses
commentary: The law specifies a form of execution for certain offenses.

title: On execution by stoning for certain offenses
commentary: Stoning is prescribed as a form of execution for particular offenses.

title: On hanging after execution for certain offenses
commentary: Post-execution display is defined for certain cases within the judicial system.

title: On burial on the same day as execution
commentary: Burial is carried out without delay, establishing a defined conclusion to judicial action.

title: On the treatment of a Hebrew servant
commentary: Specific laws define how a Hebrew servant is to be treated within the system.

title: On the rights and redemption of a female servant
commentary: A female servant is given defined rights, including provisions for redemption.

title: On allowing redemption for a female servant
commentary: Provision is made for redemption, offering a path out of servitude.

title: On the treatment of a non-Israelite servant
commentary: Different categories of servants are governed by distinct legal frameworks.

title: On liability for personal injury
commentary: Injury caused by one person to another carries defined liability within the law.

title: On liability for damage caused by an animal
commentary: Damage caused by an animal is addressed through defined responsibility of its owner.

title: On liability for damage caused by a pit
commentary: Hazards created by a person carry responsibility for resulting damage.

title: On restitution for theft
commentary: Theft is addressed through restitution, restoring loss to the injured party.

title: On liability for damage caused by grazing
commentary: Damage caused by animals grazing on another’s property incurs responsibility.

title: On liability for damage caused by fire
commentary: Damage caused by fire carries defined responsibility under the law.

title: On the responsibilities of an unpaid guardian
commentary: An unpaid guardian is governed by defined responsibilities for entrusted property.

title: On the responsibilities of a paid guardian
commentary: A paid guardian carries a different level of responsibility for entrusted property.

title: On the responsibilities of a borrower
commentary: A borrower is held responsible for what is borrowed under defined conditions.

title: On fairness in buying and selling
commentary: Transactions are governed by fairness, ensuring just dealings between parties.

title: On resolving disputes between litigants
commentary: Disputes are brought forward and resolved through established legal processes.

title: On intervening to save one pursued
commentary: Action is taken to protect a person from harm, even in urgent circumstances.

title: On the laws of inheritance
commentary: Inheritance is governed by defined rules, ensuring orderly transfer of property.
